title: Active Model B$^-$ from Mass-Conserving Reaction-Diffusion Systems
authors: Davide Toffenetti, Beatrice Nettuno, Henrik Weyer, Erwin Frey
abstract: We show that the late-time dynamics of a minimal three-component mass-conserving reaction--diffusion system reduce to a scalar active field theory, Active Model B$^-$ (AMB$^-$), in which a density-dependent interfacial coefficient $κ(φ)$ turns negative at high density. This drives a finite-wavelength instability and stabilises microphase-separated patterns, in contrast to the unbounded coarsening of two-component mass-conserving systems. Unlike Active Model B$^+$, AMB$^-$ retains a chemical potential that remains a state function, inherited from the underlying conservation law, but admits no equation of state for the pressure.
summary: This work derives Active Model B− (AMB−), an effective scalar field theory, by analyzing the late-time dynamics of a complex three-component mass-conserving reaction-diffusion system. The key finding is that AMB− exhibits a density-dependent interfacial coefficient that drives a finite-wavelength instability, stabilizing microphase separation. The methodology involves advanced field-theoretic reductions, providing a powerful framework for understanding pattern selection in non-equilibrium matter.
main_problem: The paper addresses the theoretical challenge of understanding pattern formation in mass-conserving reaction-diffusion systems, specifically aiming to derive an effective active scalar field theory that captures finite-wavelength pattern selection.
main_result: The late-time dynamics reduce to Active Model B− (AMB−), a scalar active field theory characterized by a density-dependent interfacial coefficient $\kappa(\phi)$ that turns negative at high density, stabilizing microphase-separated patterns.
method: The derivation uses adiabatic elimination and gradient expansion techniques to reduce a complex three-component mass-conserving system into a single, effective fourth-order partial differential equation.
model_or_system: The model is based on a minimal three-component mass-conserving reaction-diffusion (McRD) system involving membrane-bound and cytosolic species. The effective theory, AMB−, is a scalar active field theory derived from this underlying conservation law.
key_observables: Microphase-separated patterns, finite-wavelength instability, and the structure of the steady-state interface profiles.
important_parameters:
The reactivation rate $
u$, which interpolates between different physical regimes (e.g., coarsening vs. stable separation).
main_assumptions: The derivation relies on the system being in the late-time, diffusion-limited regime, and involves approximations like gradient expansion and quasi-steady-state elimination.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the reaction scheme, the comparison between different model limits (PFC, 2-comp, AMB−), and the stability analysis showing transitions between different types of interface profiles (monotonic, non-monotonic, etc.).
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first defining the complex three-component McRD system, then applying adiabatic elimination and gradient expansion to derive the effective AMB− equation. It analyzes the resulting steady-state ODE via linear stability analysis to characterize the stability regimes and pattern selection mechanisms.
why_interesting: While focused on classical pattern formation, the mathematical techniques used—deriving an effective, non-linear, gradient-based field theory from underlying conservation laws—are highly relevant to modeling non-equilibrium dynamics in quantum many-body systems or open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Reaction scheme of the three-component model [Eq. (1)]: the membrane-bound species m detaches at rate α(m) into the inactive cytosolic state ci, which reactivates at rate ν into the active state ca, and ca recruits back to the membrane at rate γ(m). (b) Limiting two-component mass-conserving model, obtained in the limit ν →∞, in which ci and ca collapse onto a single lumped cytosolic species c. (c) Phase-space construction for the three-component model: the reactive nullcline (gray curve) defines the local reactive equilibria, and the steady-state interface trajectory (black) is constrained to a plane of constant η (beige, light gray); the inset zooms in on the homogeneous...

Figure 2. Phase diagram of phenomenological AMB−, obtained from finite-element simulations [34] in the (¯ϕ, κ1) plane. Increasing κ1 at fixed mean density traverses a se- quence of morphologies from the PFC limit (κ1 ≲0, periodic patterns) to the Model B limit (κ1 ≳1, macrophase separa- tion), passing through coexistence of large and small droplets, microphase-separated droplets, and stripes/foams in the in- termediate regime. Colors encode local density ϕ (teal, dark gray; beige, light gray). Parameters: κ(ϕ) = tanh(κ1 −ϕ), M = 1, χ = 0.055ϕ, η∗(ϕ) = −ϕ + ϕ3, λ = 0.

Figure 3. (a) Linear stability of the homogeneous state in the (¯ϕ, κ1) plane (parameters as in Fig. 2): Type II (laven- der, dark gray) and Type I (coral, medium gray) instabili- ties; stable elsewhere (white). Symbols in 1D simulations: triangles (periodic), squares (multi-wavelength), filled/open circles (monotonic/non-monotonic interfaces). (b) Represen- tative steady-state profiles, one per symbol class. (c) Spatial- eigenvalue analysis of plateau solutions ϕ±, classified by the decay rate q: real (cream, light gray, monotonic tail), com- plex (mint, dark gray, non-monotonic tail), imaginary (coral, medium gray, plateau unstable).
